sections.subtitle: Match Overview
sections.text: Torino and Udinese face off in Round 33 of the Serie A at the Stadio Olimpico Grande Torino in Turin. Both teams are neck-and-neck in the league standings, with Torino at 11th place and Udinese at 12th, each having accumulated 44 points so far. This matchup promises to be a closely contested battle given their similar positions in the table. The referee for the game, Giuseppe Collu, averages 4.7 yellow cards per game, suggesting a potentially physical encounter.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head History
sections.text: In their last 5 encounters, Torino has dominated with 3 wins, while 2 matches ended in draws, and Udinese has failed to secure a victory. Their most recent clash on December 29, 2024, resulted in a 2-2 draw, with Torino's Che Adams earning Player of the Match honors with an impressive Sofascore rating of 8.3. Torino remains unbeaten in these head-to-heads over the past 5 matches, a streak they will look to extend.

sections.subtitle: Team Form and Streaks
sections.text: Torino comes into this match after a run of 3 games without a win, though that streak has recently ended. Udinese, on the other hand, is in poor form, currently enduring a streak of 5 games without a win and 4 consecutive losses. Both teams have struggled defensively, with Torino failing to keep a clean sheet in their last 3 games (though this streak has ended) and Udinese conceding in their last 5 matches. Additionally, Udinese has been the first to concede in 4 of their last 5 games, which could be a critical factor.

sections.subtitle: Key Players to Watch
sections.text: For Torino, Che Adams stands out as a key forward with 9 goals and 3 assists this season, overperforming his expected goals (xG) of 8.08. Another player to watch is Eljif Elmas, a midfielder with 4 goals and a notable xG overperformance of 2.6282. On the Udinese side, Oumar Solet is a standout defender, ranked 5th in the league with an average Sofascore rating of 7.33 and a high long-ball percentage rank of 5th at 9.1%. Sandi Lovrić also contributes with 2 goals and 3 assists, making him a creative force in midfield.

sections.subtitle: Missing Players Impact
sections.text: Torino will miss Nikola Vlašić, a midfielder with 5 goals and 4 assists, whose absence could affect their attacking fluidity. Udinese faces a bigger blow with key forwards Lorenzo Lucca (10 goals) and Florian Thauvin (8 goals, 3 assists) sidelined, potentially weakening their offensive threat significantly. These absences could tilt the balance in Torino’s favor, especially at home.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics Insights
sections.text: Torino has scored 39 goals this season, ranking 12th in the league, but they’ve underperformed their expected goals by -6.3304, placing them 16th in over/underperformance. They’ve conceded 40 goals, ranking 9th for least goals conceded, and managed 10 clean sheets (10th in the league). Udinese’s goal distribution shows strength in the latter stages of matches, with 20.4% of their goals scored between the 46th and 60th minutes and another 20.4% between the 76th and 90th minutes. Torino, meanwhile, peaks between the 31st and 45th minutes, scoring 25% of their goals in that period.

sections.subtitle: Standout Performers in Recent Matches
sections.text: Torino’s goalkeeper Vanja Milinković-Savić has been a consistent performer against Udinese in the last 5 matchups, starting all games with an average Sofascore rating of 7.28 and making 2.8 saves per game. For Udinese, Hassane Kamara offers a threat from midfield with 1 goal and 4 assists, while Jaka Bijol anchors the defense despite underperforming his xG of 2.01 with just 1 goal scored.

sections.subtitle: Tactical and Disciplinary Trends
sections.text: Torino commits an average of 2.17 yellow cards per game, ranking 15th for least yellow cards in the league, while their matches often see less than 4.5 cards in 8 of their last 10 games. Udinese matches also trend towards fewer cards, with 7 of their last 9 games under 4.5 cards. Both teams have played in low-scoring affairs recently, with Torino seeing under 2.5 goals in 5 of their last 6 matches and Udinese in 5 of their last 7. Torino averages 4.09 corners per game (16th in the league), indicating limited set-piece opportunities.
